label: fiction
title: The Quiet Alarm
setting: a communal apartment building, a coin-sized tracking tag, and months of silent location alerts in the present day
elements: The accused repeatedly monitored or followed the complainant
The conduct was intentional and caused reasonable fear
hook: A tracking tag travels beneath Sera Noll's bicycle seat for eighty-three days. The purchase account is shared, the fingerprints are partial, and the accused says anyone could have planted it. The shared account muddies who bought it. The battery change — on the night her bicycle sat in his repair room — points to who kept it running.

accused.human: Oris Vale, 38, repairs musical instruments and shares custody of a teenage son.
accused.if_guilty: imprisonment, a protection order affecting family contact, and the end of a small repair business run from home

prosecution.speaker: pc
prosecution.text: For eighty-three days, Oris Vale knew where Sera Noll slept, worked, and met friends because a tag beneath her bicycle reported every move. It was purchased through his household, renamed inside the account settings, and opened for a battery change while his repair-room camera recorded the bicycle beside his bench. He maintained the surveillance, and that monitoring left her reasonably afraid.

defence.speaker: dc
defence.text: The household account served four relatives and the building workshop. The tag carries mixed fingerprints, and anyone with the common stairwell code could reach the bicycle. Oris and Sera still exchanged property after separating. The prosecution has assembled ordinary contact and shared technology into intent. It cannot identify who planted the tag or who viewed most alerts.

prosecution.speaker: pc
prosecution.text: Planting and viewing leave different traces, and both converge. Oris renamed the tag Quiet Alarm, dismissed repeated safety warnings, opened its history after Sera changed routes, and replaced the battery on the only night the bicycle stood outside his repair room. The shared account creates noise around the edges; the maintenance sequence identifies deliberate control.

defence.speaker: dc
defence.text: A device history can show an account, not a human mind. The caretaker moved bicycles, relatives used the tablet, and Sera visited the repair room voluntarily to collect property. The camera never shows Oris touching the tag. If another person could still have done the battery change, you must acquit — Oris does not have to name who else did it.

epilogue: The building created locked bicycle storage and stopped sharing workshop credentials among residents. Sera changed her commuting routine again, this time by choice, and joined a support group for technology-assisted abuse. Oris moved the repair business to a commercial unit while family arrangements were reviewed separately. The device manufacturer simplified unwanted-tracking alerts and preserved warning acknowledgements for longer after advocacy groups examined the case.

beats.speaker: dc
beats.text: Exhibit one is the purchase account record for the year. Four relatives and two workshop devices used the shared household credit. The tracking tag was bought on that account. No receipt, invoice, or delivery name identifies Oris as the purchaser of the tag.
beats.reveal_note: The purchase account looked conclusive until shared household use was tested.

beats.speaker: w1
beats.text: A friend lent me a phone that alerts when an unknown tracker is nearby. It pinged under my seat; we found a coin-sized tag fixed there. For months Oris had appeared near a new gym, a friend building, and a weekend market I never mentioned. After the third coincidence I stopped telling anyone my plans. I was afraid he already knew them.
beats.reveal_note: Her sightings suggest a pattern but do not alone prove who placed or watched the tag.

beats.speaker: w1
beats.text: You and Oris still exchanged belongings during those months? Yes, through the caretaker when possible. You visited the repair room once with your bicycle? I left it outside while collecting boxes. You never saw Oris attach a tracking device? No. I saw the pattern of appearances before I knew why.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: You and Oris still exchanged belongings during those months?

turns.speaker: w1
turns.text: Yes, through the caretaker when possible.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: You visited the repair room once with your bicycle?

turns.speaker: w1
turns.text: I left it outside while collecting boxes.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: You never saw Oris attach a tracking device?

turns.speaker: w1
turns.text: No. I saw the pattern of appearances before I knew why.

beats.reveal_note: Exchanging belongings during those months explains shared access, not who maintained the tag.

beats.speaker: dc
beats.text: Exhibit two is a partial print report. Two usable fingerprint areas are mixed. One could match Oris; one is unknown. That shows someone handled the tag, not who planted or watched it. The tag passed through a household drawer and a communal workshop before recovery.
beats.reveal_note: The partial prints support handling, not who installed or monitored the tag.

beats.speaker: w2
beats.text: The account logged 312 location checks. Most came from a shared tablet, but seventeen were opened remotely after Sera deviated from her usual route. Safety warnings appeared six times and were dismissed. The user then renamed the device Quiet Alarm, language not required by the system and visible only inside settings.
beats.reveal_note: The account logged 312 location checks; volume alone does not prove who watched them.

beats.speaker: pc
beats.text: Exhibit three synchronises the battery compartment log with the repair-room camera. At 19:42 the tag loses power; Oris is already inside on his code. At 19:44 Pia Dorne's code opens the door and she wheels Sera's bicycle to his bench, then leaves. At 19:51 the tag restarts with a fresh battery. Oris remains the only person continuously present.
beats.reveal_note: The timed battery restart beside his bench was strong timing evidence of maintenance.

beats.speaker: w2
beats.text: The camera does not show the underside of the bicycle seat? No. The workbench blocks that view. A caretaker also knew the room's emergency code? Yes, but that code was not used. Could a device restart after an accidental battery movement? No. The log shows a brand-new battery with a new serial number, and a full open-and-close of the battery door — not a bump that knocked power back on.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: The camera does not show the underside of the bicycle seat?

turns.speaker: w2
turns.text: No. The workbench blocks that view.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: A caretaker also knew the room's emergency code?

turns.speaker: w2
turns.text: Yes, but that code was not used.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: Could a device restart after an accidental battery movement?

turns.speaker: w2
turns.text: No. The log shows a brand-new battery with a new serial number, and a full open-and-close of the battery door — not a bump that knocked power back on.

beats.reveal_note: The camera could not see under the seat; the battery log still ruled out an accidental bump restart.

beats.speaker: w3
beats.text: I moved the bicycle into the repair room because the corridor was being cleaned. Oris was inside alone and asked me to leave it near his bench. I used my code at 19:44, left the bicycle, and went out again. I returned about ten minutes later; he said he had adjusted the loose seat. After I left the first time, nobody else entered.
beats.reveal_note: Pia's account put Oris alone with the bicycle in the decisive window after she wheeled it in.

beats.speaker: w3
beats.text: Your access log shows an occupied room, not what Oris did at the bench? Correct. I did not see the tag or battery change. The bicycle was out of your sight for ten minutes? Yes, and nobody else entered. So you speak from access records, not from seeing the act? Yes. He had the chance. I did not see him touch the tag.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: Your access log shows an occupied room, not what Oris did at the bench?

turns.speaker: w3
turns.text: Correct. I did not see the tag or battery change.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: The bicycle was out of your sight for ten minutes?

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: So you speak from access records, not from seeing the act?

turns.speaker: w3
turns.text: Yes. He had the chance. I did not see him touch the tag.

beats.reveal_note: Pia could fix opportunity after she brought the bicycle in; she could not describe the hidden act.

beats.speaker: pc
beats.text: Exhibit four is a deleted note recovered from Oris's personal tablet. It lists Sera's new gym hours beside the tag's private identifier and the words battery due Sunday. The note was created after the fifth safety warning and edited three minutes after a remote location check, using a profile unavailable to other household members.
beats.reveal_note: The deleted note on Oris's personal profile tied gym hours to a battery due date.

beats.speaker: judge
beats.text: The judge directs you that Oris need not show who else could access the purchase account or repair room. The prosecution must prove, beyond reasonable doubt, that Oris intentionally monitored Sera with the tracker and that this caused reasonable fear. Shared access is not enough by itself. Do not search for the device, residence, routes, or people outside the admitted evidence.
beats.reveal_note: The judge reminded the jury that shared access and suspicion are not enough without proof of knowing monitoring and reasonable fear.

twist: The shared account could not say who bought or first handled the tag. The battery did: it died while Sera's bicycle stood beside Oris's private bench, restarted seven minutes later, and was paired with a note on his tablet naming her gym hours and its next replacement date. The surveillance lasted because he returned to maintain it.

jurors.label: Juror 2
jurors.persona: A foreperson who restates disagreements before calling a vote.

agree: For me, the anchor is the battery serial, repair-room interval, and private note — they answer the weakness that first divided this room.
In my notes at seat 2, the battery serial, repair-room interval, and private note agree across sources that did not depend on one another.
pushback: I will not let the shared purchase account and partial print decide more than their source can support.
I need the chronology laid out before I choose between those accounts. Here that means shared ownership against private maintenance.
concede: I admit the shared purchase account and partial print looked damning at first, especially before the underlying record was opened.
final: My verdict rests on the battery serial, repair-room interval, and private note, with the burden left where the judge placed it.
